     6|tags: [demand, demand-letter, preparation, records, treatment-complete, phase-change]
     7|confidence: low
     8|evidence_count: 3
     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - medical-records-collection
    13|  - case-phase-transitions
    14|  - attorney-review-triggers
    15|  - negotiation-after-demand
    16|---
    17|
    18|# Demand Preparation Workflow
    19|
    20|> The demand phase begins when the client's treatment is complete and all medical records/bills have been collected. The transition to "Demand in Progress" is a formal phase change that triggers a series of tasks: final records requests, records review for completeness and relatedness, lien identification, and ultimately the drafting and sending of the demand package to the insurance adjuster.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|### Prerequisites for Demand Phase Entry
    25|1. Client confirms treatment is complete (via phone call, documented in log)
    26|2. All known medical providers have been identified
    27|3. Outstanding records/bills requests have been sent
    28|4. Attorney or case manager authorizes the phase change
    29|
    30|### Demand Preparation Sequence
    31|1. **Phase Change**: Status changed from "Treatment" to "Demand in Progress" with reason logged
    32|2. **Final Records Requests**: Any outstanding records/bills are requested or re-requested
    33|3. **Records Review**: Medical records are reviewed for:
    34|   - Relatedness to the accident (unrelated treatment must be separated)
    35|   - Completeness (all visits documented)
    36|   - Referrals to other providers discovered in records (new providers must be added)
    37|4. **Lien Identification**: Health insurance liens identified and lien amounts requested (e.g., from Rawlings/WellCare)
    38|5. **Demand Drafting**: Attorney prepares the demand package
    39|6. **Demand Sent**: Package sent to the BI adjuster; follow-up task created
    40|
    41|### Records Review Reveals New Providers
    42|A critical pattern: when records from one provider are received and reviewed, they may reference visits to other providers not previously on file. These must be added and their records requested before the demand can proceed.
    43|
    44|### Adjuster Proactive Contact During Demand Prep
    45|Insurance adjusters may proactively call to check on claim status during the demand preparation period, especially as the case approaches SOL. This should be documented and the adjuster given a general update without committing to specific timelines.
    46|
    47|## Evidence
    48|
    49|- In an auto accident case (DOI 2/2023), the client confirmed treatment was complete during a phone call on 3/11/2024 ("done treating with all medical providers listed on file. No more hospital visits."). Phase was changed to "Demand in Progress" that same day. Records review revealed a referral to White House Clinics and King's Eye Care not previously on file — both had to be added and records requested. Lien request was sent to The Rawlings Company (WellCare) during this phase. The BI adjuster (Clearcover) proactively called to follow up on claim status.
    50|- In the same case, records from providers were separated for relatedness (unrelated visits excluded) before demand preparation.
    51|- The demand was eventually sent, but the case then moved to "Settlement" and then back to "Negotiation" when the client refused the offer, indicating the demand-to-settlement pipeline is not always linear.
    52|
    53|## Agent Guidance
    54|
    55|1. When a client reports treatment is complete, confirm via phone and document the call
    56|2. Trigger the phase change to "Demand in Progress" and log who authorized it
    57|3. Immediately create tasks for any outstanding records/bills requests
    58|4. When records arrive, review them for references to other providers — add any new providers found
    59|5. Separate records by relatedness to the accident
    60|6. Send final health insurance lien requests (to Rawlings, Optum, etc.) to get lien amounts
    61|7. Compile all records, bills, and lien information for the attorney's demand preparation
    62|8. When an adjuster calls proactively, document the contact and provide a general update
    63|9. After demand is sent, create a 30-day follow-up task for adjuster response
    64|
    65|## Pitfalls
    66|
    67|- Moving to demand before all providers are identified (records review may reveal new ones)
    68|- Not separating related vs. unrelated treatment in the medical records
    69|- Forgetting to request health insurance lien amounts before the demand
    70|- Not documenting the client's confirmation that treatment is complete
    71|- Failing to follow up with the adjuster after demand is sent
    72|- Assuming the demand-to-settlement path is linear (client may reject the offer)
    73|
    74|## Related
    75|
    76|- [[medical-records-collection]] — records must be complete before demand
    77|- [[case-phase-transitions]] — demand is a formal phase change with prerequisites
    78|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — attorney reviews and approves the demand
    79|- [[negotiation-after-demand]] — what happens after the demand is sent
    80|", "total_lines": 79, "file_size": 4890, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    17|
    18|# Negotiation After Demand
    19|
    20|> After a demand is sent, the case enters negotiation with the insurance adjuster. This phase involves waiting for and evaluating the initial offer, communicating with the client about the offer, potential counter-offers, and either settlement or escalation to litigation. The negotiation phase can be complicated by client dissatisfaction, offer rejection, and lien resolution.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|### Post-Demand Sequence
    25|1. **Demand Sent**: Follow-up task created for adjuster response
    26|2. **Adjuster Response**: Initial offer received (or adjuster requests more time/information)
    27|3. **Attorney Evaluation**: Attorney evaluates the offer against case value, medical specials, liens
    28|4. **Client Communication**: Client informed of offer and attorney recommendation
    29|5. **Decision Point**: Accept, counter-offer, or reject and prepare for litigation
    30|
    31|### Client Rejection Pattern
    32|A significant pattern emerges when clients reject settlement offers:
    33|- The case may be labeled "Settlement" prematurely (before client accepts)
    34|- Client calls in asking for an update, is told the case is "on settlement"
    35|- Client explicitly rejects the offer ("I am not settling")
    36|- The case must be re-classified from "Settlement" back to "Negotiation"
    37|- This back-and-forth creates frustration for all parties
    38|
    39|### Adjuster Follow-Up
    40|- Adjusters may need to be prompted for a response after demand
    41|- The firm sends follow-up communications to the adjuster
    42|- Lien amounts must be resolved during this phase to calculate the client's net recovery
    43|
    44|### Communication Challenges
    45|- Clients in negotiation become increasingly frustrated with the pace
    46|- Missed calls and voicemails escalate in urgency and frustration
    47|- Clients may threaten to "take another route" (change attorneys)
    48|- Regular, proactive communication is critical during this phase
    49|
    50|## Evidence
    51|
    52|- In an auto accident case, the demand was sent and the case moved to "Settlement" phase. However, the client explicitly refused the offer — a voicemail stated "I told you I am not settling for what you are... so come back." The phase had to be changed from "Settlement" back to "Negotiation." Staff confusion was evident: one team member noted "appears was settled but client didn't accept so maybe ongoing negotiations."
    53|- In the same case, the client's frustration escalated over months: text messages included "Hey are you going to get back with me or do I need to take another route. Let me know today" (6/2024) and multiple missed calls with voicemails demanding updates (3/2025, 8/2025, 10/2025, 2/2026, 3/2026). The attorney requested the adjuster pay a lien from Rawlings (10/2025), indicating lien resolution was happening during negotiation.
    54|- The case demonstrated a pattern where the firm told the client the case was "on settlement phase" and "we can't disclose the amount yet" — which created client frustration when the settlement did not materialize quickly.
    55|
    56|## Agent Guidance
    57|
    58|1. After demand is sent, do NOT prematurely label the case as "Settlement" until the client has agreed to accept an offer
    59|2. When an offer is received, schedule a call with the client to discuss — do not communicate offers via text
    60|3. If the client rejects an offer, document the rejection clearly and move the case back to "Negotiation"
    61|4. Maintain regular contact during negotiation (at minimum every 2 weeks)
    62|5. When the client asks for updates, provide specific information about what is happening and what's next
    63|6. During negotiation, resolve liens in parallel — don't wait until after settlement to address them
    64|7. If the client threatens to leave, escalate to the attorney immediately for a personal call
    65|8. Track every client contact attempt (successful or missed) in the activity log
    66|
    67|## Pitfalls
    68|
    69|- Premature phase change to "Settlement" before client agreement
    70|- Telling the client the case is in "settlement" when it's actually in negotiation (creates false expectations)
    71|- Not communicating offer details to the client promptly
    72|- Allowing weeks/months of silence during active negotiation
    73|- Staff not knowing the current case status when client calls in
    74|- Not resolving liens during negotiation (delays final settlement)
    75|- Client losing trust due to poor communication (may terminate representation)
    76|
    77|## Related
    78|
    79|- [[demand-preparation-workflow]] — the demand that precedes negotiation
    80|- [[client-expectations-management]] — managing client expectations during the long negotiation phase
    81|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — all offers require attorney evaluation
    82|- [[statute-of-limitations-monitoring]] — SOL may force a decision during negotiation
    83|", "total_lines": 82, "file_size": 5048, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    17|
    18|# Health Insurance Lien Management
    19|
    20|> When a PI client has health insurance that pays accident-related medical bills, the health insurer asserts a subrogation lien — a right to be reimbursed from any settlement. Managing these liens involves verifying their accuracy, removing unrelated charges, and ultimately negotiating a reduction. Liens directly reduce the client's net recovery.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|The health insurance lien lifecycle:
    25|
    26|1. **Identification**: The health insurer sends a "notice of subrogation claim" or "subrogation interest" letter once they learn of the accident
    27|2. **Itemization Request**: Request a detailed itemization of the lien showing each date of service and amount paid
    28|3. **Accuracy Audit**: Compare the lien itemization against the case's actual accident-related treatment
    29|4. **Dispute Unrelated Charges**: If the lien includes charges for treatment unrelated to the accident (pre-existing conditions, unrelated medical visits), request removal with supporting documentation
    30|5. **Updated Ledger**: Once disputed items are removed, obtain an updated lien ledger
    31|6. **Settlement Integration**: Factor the adjusted lien into the settlement math
    32|7. **Negotiation**: After settlement, negotiate a reduction of the lien (typically firms negotiate a percentage reduction)
    33|
    34|## Evidence
    35|
    36|- In Case C, the health insurer (Anthem) asserted a subrogation lien that included a 5/16/2024 date of service for an angiogram. The attorney identified that this procedure was scheduled prior to the accident (on 4/10/2024) for a pre-existing cardiac condition and was unrelated to the MVA. The paralegal emailed the lien handler (Carelon) requesting removal of the unrelated dates of service. An updated lien ledger with the charges removed was received the same day.
    37|- In Case D (chain-reaction MVA with 20+ providers), the case accumulated 6+ liens including health insurance (Optum at $1,707), provider liens (Cutting Edge Orthopedics at $25,763.75), and a hospital funding lien (Plano Surgical Hospital Funding LLC at $2,553.70). Multiple "Unknown" lien holders were also identified, illustrating that lien tracking becomes exponentially harder with more providers. Provider liens (as distinct from health insurance liens) may come from providers who treated on a lien basis.
    38|- In Case F (auto accident, dental injury), Anthem Blue Cross Blue Shield and Anthem Medicaid both asserted liens of $425.41 on the same case. The firm used ZipLiens to track the health insurance lien but initially sent the request to the wrong entity. Delta Dental of Kentucky also asserted a lien (amount unconfirmed). This demonstrates that dental injury cases can involve dental insurance liens in addition to health insurance liens — a distinct lien type not seen in standard MVA cases.
    39|- In Case G (auto accident, UIM litigation), Humana asserted a health insurance subrogation lien of $11,809. The case also involved pre-settlement funding ($31,200 from Legal Funding Partners), illustrating how the combination of health insurance liens plus funding can dramatically reduce client net recovery.
    40|- In Case H (auto accident, Medicaid), a Conduent lien of $20,669.08 was identified. Conduent is the third-party administrator for Kentucky Medicaid subrogation. The firm sent a first lien letter to Conduent at intake and later provided the Conduent lien information to the KAC adjuster during the claims process. Conduent called the firm to follow up on the case status, demonstrating that Medicaid subrogation administrators actively monitor cases.
    41|
    42|## Agent Guidance
    43|
    44|1. When a subrogation notice arrives, log it immediately and request a detailed itemization
    45|2. Compare every date of service on the lien against the client's accident-related treatment dates
    46|3. Flag any dates of service that fall outside the treatment window or relate to unrelated conditions
    47|4. Draft a dispute letter to the lien handler citing the specific dates and reasons for removal
    48|5. Obtain pre-accident medical records if needed to prove a condition was pre-existing
    49|6. Keep the updated lien ledger in the case file for settlement calculations
    50|7. Alert the attorney whenever the lien amount changes materially
    51|
    52|## Pitfalls
    53|
    54|- Accepting the lien amount at face value without auditing it
    55|- Not knowing the client's pre-existing medical history (makes it impossible to identify unrelated charges)
    56|- Delaying the lien dispute — the longer you wait, the harder it is to resolve before settlement
    57|- Confusing the health insurance lien with PIP payments (they are separate obligations)
    58|- Not getting the updated lien ledger in writing after charges are removed
    59|
    60|### Government vs. Private Liens
    61|
    62|Note: Government entity liens (VA, Medicare, Medicaid) follow fundamentally different processes than private health insurance liens. See [[va-lien-management]] for the VA-specific lien process. Key differences include federal legal authority, longer timelines, and less flexibility in negotiation.
    63|
    64|## Related
    65|
    66|- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP payments reduce what health insurance pays (and thus the lien)
    67|- [[demand-value-factors]] — the lien amount affects net recovery and settlement strategy
    68|- [[pre-existing-condition-separation]] — separating accident-related from pre-existing treatment
    69|- [[va-lien-management]] — VA liens are a distinct category requiring different handling
    70|", "total_lines": 69, "file_size": 5799, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    16|
    17|# Client Frustration Drives Premature Settlement or Attorney Attrition
    18|
    19|> When clients become frustrated with the pace and communication during the negotiation phase, two negative outcomes are likely: (1) the client pressures for a premature settlement at a lower value than the case warrants, or (2) the client threatens to or actually changes attorneys. Both outcomes are preventable through proactive communication and clear expectation-setting.
    20|
    21|## Connection
    22|
    23|**[[client-frustration-escalation]]** documents the 4-stage escalation pattern from polite inquiry to threats and distrust.
    24|
    25|**[[client-expectations-management]]** provides the proactive communication strategies that prevent escalation.
    26|
    27|**[[negotiation-after-demand]]** shows how premature "settlement" labeling and poor offer communication create client confusion and frustration.
    28|
    29|## Insight
    30|
    31|In the observed auto accident case, the client was told the case was "on settlement phase" but then refused the offer. Over the following 12+ months, the client made dozens of calls (many missed), sent frustrated text messages, and explicitly threatened to "take another route." Staff members were unsure of the case status ("appears was settled but client didn't accept"). The combination of poor communication, premature settlement labeling, and missed calls created a situation where the client-firm relationship was severely strained.
    32|
    33|In the premises liability case (6+ years in litigation), the client texted the attorney directly after a court appearance with no follow-up. The attorney's prompt, substantive response prevented escalation — demonstrating that responsive communication, even when there's limited news, maintains the relationship.
    34|
    35|## Agent Guidance
    36|
    37|1. Never label a case as "Settlement" until the client has agreed to accept the offer
    38|2. When an offer is received, schedule a call with the client within 48 hours to discuss
    39|3. Maintain 2-week check-in schedule during active negotiation
    40|4. When a client shows Stage 2 frustration signs, escalate to the attorney for a personal call within 24 hours
    41|5. Ensure all staff members know the current case status so they can answer client questions coherently
    42|", "total_lines": 41, "file_size": 2656, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    17|
    18|# Premises Liability Cases Require Litigation Preparation from Day One
    19|
    20|> Premises liability cases are significantly more likely to proceed to litigation than auto accident cases. This means that from the earliest stages of file setup, the team should prepare for litigation: requesting certified (not standard) medical records, budgeting for expert evaluations, and preserving evidence from the property owner. The failure to prepare for litigation at intake creates costly delays when the case inevitably moves to suit.
    21|
    22|## Connection
    23|
    24|**[[premises-liability-case-patterns]]** establishes that PL cases have a different trajectory than auto accident cases, with a much higher likelihood of proceeding to litigation against property owners with dedicated defense counsel.
    25|
    26|**[[litigation-process-patterns]]** documents the specific requirements of litigation: certified records, expert reports, discovery compliance, and court deadlines.
    27|
    28|**[[medical-records-collection]]** shows that litigation requires certified copies of records — requesting standard copies initially and then having to re-request certified copies wastes months.
    29|
    30|**[[expert-reports-and-evaluations]]** shows that FCE, IME, and vocational reports are commonly needed in PL litigation, and their scheduling and coordination adds months to the case timeline.
    31|
    32|## Insight
    33|
    34|In the observed PL case, the case spanned 6+ years from incident to present, involving complaint filing, discovery, depositions, mediation, and multiple expert evaluations. The team had to re-request records as certified copies for litigation and coordinate multiple expert evaluations that were rescheduled repeatedly. Had the team anticipated litigation from the start, certified records could have been requested initially, and expert evaluations could have been scheduled earlier in the process.
    35|
    36|## Agent Guidance
    37|
    38|1. For all premises liability cases, request CERTIFIED records from the start (even before litigation is filed)
    39|2. Send preservation letters to property owners at intake (incident reports, surveillance footage, maintenance logs)
    40|3. Budget for expert evaluations (FCE, IME, economist) at file setup, not after litigation is filed
    41|4. Track litigation-specific deadlines from the moment a complaint is filed
    42|5. Coordinate expert scheduling early — availability is limited and rescheduling is common
    43|", "total_lines": 42, "file_size": 2854, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    16|
    17|# Records Completion Gates Demand Preparation
    18|
    19|> This connection documents how delays in [[medical-records-collection]] create the primary bottleneck preventing [[case-phase-transitions]] from Treatment/Demand in Progress to Negotiation. The demand cannot be sent until records are complete, and records delays cascade into client frustration.
    20|
    21|## Connection
    22|
    23|The dependency chain:
    24|
    25|1. **Treatment Completion**: Client finishes treatment (or decides to stop)
    26|2. **Records Requests**: Firm sends records/billing requests to all providers
    27|3. **Wait Period**: Providers take 1-6+ weeks to respond
    28|4. **Completeness Check**: All providers must have returned both records AND bills
    29|5. **Demand Preparation**: Only after completeness can the demand be drafted
    30|6. **Attorney Review**: Attorney reviews and approves demand
    31|7. **Demand Sent**: Case moves to Negotiation
    32|
    33|Any delay at step 3 delays everything downstream. And the client experiences the entire wait as "nothing is happening."
    34|
    35|## Evidence
    36|
    37|- In Case B, treatment was confirmed complete in late October 2025. Records from St. Elizabeth came back same day, but records from Christ Hospital Physicians took significantly longer. The case was still in "Demand in Progress" as of March 2026 — nearly 5 months after treatment ended — partly due to records delays.
    38|- In Case C, the attorney flagged that "we need a record and was sent to get this a month ago with no action" — records delays directly prevented negotiation progress.
    39|- In Case B, the paralegal told the client's husband: "I'm still waiting for the complete records/billing from Christ Hospital Physicians Primary Care. Once I have both of those I can move this case towards resolution." This directly connects records delays to case resolution timeline.
    40|
    41|## Insight
    42|
    43|Records collection is the single biggest controllable bottleneck in case lifecycle. Reducing the average records turnaround from 4-6 weeks to 2 weeks would compress the overall case timeline by months.
    44|
    45|## Agent Guidance
    46|
    47|1. Send records requests the same day treatment is confirmed complete (or earlier if possible)
    48|2. Use the fastest channel available for each provider (email for some, fax for others)
    49|3. Set aggressive follow-up intervals: 7 days for the first follow-up, then every 7 days
    50|4. Maintain a per-case tracker showing each provider's records status and bill status
    51|5. When the attorney asks about case status, lead with the records tracker
    52|6. When the client asks about timeline, explain which specific records are outstanding
    53|
    54|## Related
    55|
    56|- [[medical-records-collection]] — the process being optimized
    57|- [[case-phase-transitions]] — the transitions that records completion unlocks
    58|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — stalled records should trigger attorney escalation
    59|", "total_lines": 58, "file_size": 3170, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    15|
    16|# Client Communication Through Family Members
    17|
    18|> In many PI cases, the primary point of contact is a family member (spouse, parent) rather than the injured client directly. This pattern creates both efficiencies (an engaged advocate) and risks (communication gaps, authorization requirements). Managing these relationships requires clear protocols around authorization, documentation, and expectation-setting.
    19|
    20|## Pattern
    21|
    22|Two common scenarios emerge:
    23|
    24|**Spouse as Primary Contact**: The spouse handles all communication, forwards medical documents, asks status questions, and relays information to the injured client. The injured client may have signed the retainer but is less directly involved in day-to-day case management.
    25|
    26|**Parent with POA**: When a client is incarcerated or otherwise unavailable, a parent with Power of Attorney may serve as the authorized representative. The POA document must be obtained and verified before the firm can discuss case details.
    27|
    28|In both scenarios, the family member tends to:
    29|- Be more proactive about asking for updates
    30|- Forward bills and medical documents as they arrive
    31|- Express urgency about resolution ("we want this to go away")
    32|- Ask about the impact of unpaid bills on credit
    33|
    34|## Evidence
    35|
    36|- In Case B, the client's husband was the primary communicator throughout the entire case. He forwarded all medical bills, relayed appointment results, asked about case status repeatedly, and expressed concern about bills going to collections. The client herself was largely absent from communications.
    37|- In Case A, the client was incarcerated and the client's mother (with POA) was the sole point of contact. The mother provided intake information, the POA document, and followed up on case status.
    38|- In Case H (MVA, air evac), the client designated a go-between ("Anna"/Ann Dattillo) as the intermediary for all communications. The firm was instructed to communicate through Anna "per Client instructions." Anna was added as an associated contact. She requested copies of the deposition and case updates. When the attorney changed (Bryce Koon departed), the new attorney had to re-establish the communication chain through Anna. The client herself called separately, creating confusion about who was the primary contact.
    39|- In Case G (MVA, companion cases), the client was the father managing his own case AND his daughter's companion case. He needed to coordinate daughter's medical records, hospital visits, and communicate on her behalf. Multiple calls over months tried to get the companion's hospital records — the father was asked to speak to the daughter and obtain them, but this remained incomplete for 6+ months.
    40|- In Case H (MVA), initial intake was through a witness/companion ("Robert Grant") who called in. He was given the office number and told the client's case manager name. He provided pictures and information on behalf of the client. This shows that in some cases, even the initial referral comes through a third party.
    41|
    42|## Agent Guidance
    43|
    44|1. During intake, identify who the primary point of contact will be
    45|2. If it's a family member, verify authorization: POA for non-spouse, general authorization for spouse
    46|3. Document the authorized contact in the case file with their contact info
    47|4. Set expectations with the family member about case timeline and the process
    48|5. When the family member forwards bills/records, acknowledge receipt promptly and explain next steps
    49|6. When the family member expresses urgency, respond with specific status updates rather than vague reassurances
    50|7. If bills are going to collections, explain the process (PIP submission, timeline) proactively
    51|
    52|## Pitfalls
    53|
    54|- Discussing case details with a family member who has not been properly authorized
    55|- Assuming the family member is accurately relaying information to/from the client
    56|- Not setting expectations with the family member about realistic case timelines
    57|- Ignoring repeated follow-up inquiries from family members (this erodes trust)
    58|- Not having a clear protocol for who can authorize settlement decisions
    59|
    60|## Related
    61|
    62|- [[client-expectations-management]] — managing expectations is crucial when family members push for faster resolution
    63|- [[intake-via-referral]] — cases often come through family referrals
    64|", "total_lines": 63, "file_size": 4673, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Case Manager Handoff Patterns
     3|slug: case-manager-handoff-patterns
     4|phase: [phase_0_onboarding, phase_1_file_setup, phase_2_treatment, phase_4_negotiation]
     5|category: process
     6|tags: [handoff, case-manager, staff-turnover, continuity, transition]
     7|confidence: low
     8|evidence_count: 2
     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - client-frustration-escalation
    13|  - adjuster-reassignment-patterns
    14|  - intake-and-onboarding-workflow
    15|---
    16|
    17|# Case Manager Handoff Patterns
    18|
    19|> PI cases frequently outlast the tenure of the case manager who initially worked them. When a case manager leaves or cases are reassigned, the incoming staff member must pick up an unfamiliar file, establish rapport with the client, and continue without losing momentum. These handoffs create a risk of dropped tasks, communication gaps, and client frustration.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|### Handoff Triggers
    24|1. **Staff turnover**: A case manager leaves the firm and their cases are redistributed
    25|2. **Workload rebalancing**: Cases are reassigned for caseload management
    26|3. **Specialization**: A case moves to a phase requiring a different skill set (e.g., from treatment monitoring to demand preparation)
    27|
    28|### Observable Signals of Handoff
    29|- Change in the staff name on emails and call logs (e.g., correspondence shifts from one paralegal to another)
    30|- New case manager may need to request basic information already in the file (e.g., which franchise location the client worked at)
    31|- Communication gaps during the transition period
    32|- Client may not be formally introduced to the new case manager
    33|
    34|### Handoff Risks
    35|1. **Knowledge loss**: The outgoing case manager's institutional knowledge of the case (adjuster relationship, client preferences, pending issues) is not fully transferred
    36|2. **Task gaps**: Pending follow-ups may be dropped during the transition
    37|3. **Client confusion**: The client suddenly hears from a different person without explanation
    38|4. **Duplicate work**: The new case manager may re-request information already obtained
    39|
    40|### Best Practice (Observed)
    41|- Some cases show smooth handoffs where the new case manager CCs the prior one during the transition period
    42|- Filevine project email addresses maintain continuity regardless of case manager changes
    43|- The new case manager should review the full activity log before first client contact
    44|
    45|## Evidence
    46|
    47|- In a workplace injury case, the initial case manager handled the file from intake (2/2024) through approximately mid-2024. A second paralegal took over and continued through 2026. The handoff was visible in the activity log as the corresponding name on emails changed. The new case manager had to ask the client basic questions already answered in the file (e.g., the specific location where they worked), suggesting incomplete knowledge transfer.
    48|- In multiple cases, the same paralegal (Sarena) handled the case from intake through years of treatment/litigation, only for a different staff member (Bryce) to take over later phases. The CC pattern during transition helped maintain continuity.
    49|
    50|## Agent Guidance
    51|
    52|1. When taking over a case, read the last 3 months of activity logs before contacting anyone
    53|2. Send an introduction email to the client: "My name is [X], I'll be working on your case going forward"
    54|3. Review all pending tasks and follow-up dates — assume nothing was done during the gap
    55|4. Check for any adjuster contacts that need to be re-engaged
    56|5. Verify the case's current phase and landmarks against what's actually been completed
    57|6. Do not ask the client for information already documented in the file
    58|7. If possible, have the outgoing case manager provide a verbal briefing on each case
    59|
    60|## Pitfalls
    61|
    62|- Not reading the case history before contacting the client or adjuster
    63|- Asking the client for information already in the file (damages credibility)
    64|- Dropping pending follow-ups during the transition
    65|- Not introducing yourself to the client (they may not know who you are)
    66|- Losing track of the adjuster relationship built by the prior case manager
    67|
    68|## Related
    69|
    70|- [[client-frustration-escalation]] — handoffs during active phases can trigger client frustration
    71|- [[adjuster-reassignment-patterns]] — similar disruption pattern on the carrier side
    72|- [[intake-and-onboarding-workflow]] — handoffs are most disruptive when they occur near intake
    73|", "total_lines": 72, "file_size": 4320, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

-- In Case D (3-vehicle chain-reaction MVA), the firm managed separate BI claims against West Bend Mutual Insurance, Progressive Insurance Company, and American Transit Insurance, plus PIP claims against Farmers Insurance and American Transit. West Bend accepted 100% liability within 2 weeks, while the other carriers moved at different speeds. West Bend's first offer (~$13K) was rejected, leading to litigation. Meanwhile, Progressive's adjuster initiated contact for settlement discussions approximately 2.5 years later. By late 2025, the adjuster from Progressive was repeatedly calling and leaving voicemails to discuss settlement — while the West Bend litigation was in active discovery with IMEs and depositions. The case also involved a 3rd-party vehicle claim with USAA (no demand). Each carrier required separate tracking.
+- In Case D (3-vehicle chain-reaction MVA), the firm managed separate BI claims against West Bend Mutual Insurance, Progressive Insurance Company, and American Transit Insurance, plus PIP claims against Farmers Insurance and American Transit. West Bend accepted 100% liability within 2 weeks, while the other carriers moved at different speeds. West Bend's first offer (~$13K) was rejected, leading to litigation. Meanwhile, Progressive's adjuster initiated contact for settlement discussions approximately 2.5 years later. By late 2025, the adjuster from Progressive was repeatedly calling and leaving voicemails to discuss settlement — while the West Bend litigation was in active discovery with IMEs and depositions. The case also involved a 3rd-party vehicle claim with USAA (no demand). Each carrier required separate tracking.
+- In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff), the firm managed three BI claims (Liberty Mutual, State Farm, and a third carrier) plus a PIP claim with Liberty Mutual — four total insurance tracks for a single client. Policy limits were tendered quickly (25/50 split) but had to be divided between four injured plaintiffs represented by different law firms. The demand was sent to both BI carriers on the same day. The distribution negotiation between co-plaintiffs' counsel stalled progress for months, ultimately triggering litigation when the parties could not agree on allocation. This illustrates that multi-carrier complexity is compounded when multiple plaintiffs share the same policy limits.

The client exhibited a pattern of frequent missed calls (20+ over 7 months) with periodic attorney return calls.

The client also asked about deposition scheduling details and case status, suggesting the frustration was compounded by not understanding the WC litigation process.", "new_string": "- In a workers' compensation case (workplace injury, treatment phase), the client sent emails with increasingly urgent subject lines: \"ANY UPDATES?\" (8/2025) and \"UPDATES??\" (10/2025). The escalating punctuation and capitalization follow the same trajectory as the phone-based pattern. The firm responded to each, but the repeated pattern over months confirms that even cases in active treatment (not just negotiation) can trigger client frustration when the client perceives a lack of forward progress. The client also asked about deposition scheduling details and case status, suggesting the frustration was compounded by not understanding the WC litigation process.\n- In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff, negotiation/litigation), the client's frustration followed a textbook escalation: (1) polite inquiry phase with regular updates from the firm (Aug-Dec 2023), (2) frustration peaked when the client was told \"we are still waiting on [another plaintiff's] medical bills\" repeatedly over months (Jan-Apr 2024), (3) the client called to complain about \"not receiving call backs or being told conflicting information repeatedly\" — she came to the office in person to demand an update (Feb 2024), (4) the attorney spoke with her directly and calmed the situation. The pattern continued in the litigation phase with multiple missed calls (Sep-Nov 2025), text message difficulties (\"Can u you give me a call it won't lemme call you\"), and the attorney texting back and forth to coordinate. This case uniquely shows frustration driven by inter-counsel coordination delays — the client's case was ready but held hostage by another firm's inaction.\n- In Case N (premises liability, estate case, 5+ year litigation), the surviving spouse called for updates repeatedly throughout 2025-2026. The pattern showed: July 2025, the spouse called saying he \"wants to speak with attorney\" and \"has not rec'd a return call.\" The firm provided an update via the paralegal. Missed calls continued through Sep 2025, Oct 2025 (two missed calls same day), Dec 2025 (two more missed calls), and Mar 2026. This demonstrates that estate cases, where the primary contact is a surviving family member rather than the injured party, follow the same frustration escalation. The emotional dimension is heightened — the spouse is dealing with both grief and the legal process. The 5+ year timeline makes sustained communication even more critical."}

The pattern continued in the litigation phase with multiple missed calls (Sep-Nov 2025), text message difficulties ("Can u you give me a call it won't lemme call you"), and the attorney texting back and forth to coordinate. This case uniquely shows frustration driven by inter-counsel coordination delays — the client's case was ready but held hostage by another firm's inaction.
+- In Case N (premises liability, estate case, 5+ year litigation), the surviving spouse called for updates repeatedly throughout 2025-2026. The pattern showed: July 2025, the spouse called saying he "wants to speak with attorney" and "has not rec'd a return call." The firm provided an update via the paralegal. Missed calls continued through Sep 2025, Oct 2025 (two missed calls same day), Dec 2025 (two more missed calls), and Mar 2026. This demonstrates that estate cases, where the primary contact is a surviving family member rather than the injured party, follow the same frustration escalation. The emotional dimension is heightened — the spouse is dealing with both grief and the legal process. The 5+ year timeline makes sustained communication even more critical.

{"content": "    70|- In Case I (MVA, Jefferson Circuit Court), complaint filed ~8 months post-accident (24-CI-005774) due to disputed liability (BI only accepting 25%). Discovery followed, with defense counsel reviewing dash cam video. Depositions were scheduled via Zoom for both the client and a witness (companion passenger). Client nearly missed deposition — required multiple urgent texts ("Cecil, you good?", "Cecil you ready?", "Cecil?") before responding. Deposition completed successfully. Defense counsel served interrogatories and additional discovery.
    71|- In Case F (WC case), litigation followed a different track — DWC (Department of Workers' Compensation) proceedings with BRC (Benefit Review Conferences) scheduled telephonically. Defense counsel (Pohl & Aubrey) exchanged correspondence about Form 106 requests and IME scheduling. This WC litigation model is distinct from civil circuit court litigation.
    72|- In Case F (auto accident, litigation), discovery involved interrogatories and requests for production exchanged between plaintiff and defendant. Defense counsel (Dilbeck & Myers) engaged actively in scheduling depositions. The defendant's deposition was scheduled via Zoom, with the plaintiff's deposition to follow immediately after. The firm communicated with the client via text and phone to prepare for deposition. Defense counsel's responses to production requests were tracked through email exchanges.
    73|- In Case G (auto accident, UIM litigation), a UIM complaint was filed separately against the client's own insurer (Allstate) in Fayette Circuit Court. The insurer filed an answer to the complaint. Discovery commenced with the plaintiff's first set of discovery. The UIM adjuster received a courtesy copy of the complaint. This demonstrates that UIM litigation follows a parallel track to BI claims.
    74|- In Case H (auto accident, litigation with stolen vehicle), the case (22-CI-002878) involved multiple defendants and defense counsel from different firms. Key litigation events included: defense counsel requesting and receiving a 60-day extension to answer the complaint while attempting to get the insurer to accept coverage; an Examination Under Oath (EUO) of the defendant regarding the stolen vehicle claim; deposition scheduling disputes where defense counsel was "adamant" about timing constraints; unilateral scheduling of depositions when counsel could not agree; and audio/video recording requests from the court. The case lasted 4+ years from DOI through ongoing litigation.
    75|- In a PL case (Jefferson Circuit Court, DOI 9/2023) transferred from prior counsel, complaint was filed on the same day as file setup due to SOL within 60 days. Multiple defendants were named (individual parties + a commercial carrier LLC). Service of process required coordination with an out-of-state sheriff (Floyd County, IN) with associated fees. Multiple defense counsel from separate law firms entered appearances. Records requests were sent to 15+ providers simultaneously with discovery. The case generated heavy correspondence including call summaries with defense counsel. Defense counsel coordinated motion practice within weeks of filing.
    76|- In a dog bite case (Jefferson Circuit Court, DOI 8/2022), the case involved a minor client with guardian as contact. Homeowner's insurance was identified through KY Farm Bureau. After the BI claim settled, a separate complaint was filed. Discovery, depositions (of both guardian and minor client), and defense counsel rescheduling occurred. Deposition was cancelled and rescheduled multiple times by defense counsel. A jury trial date and final pretrial conference were set. The court system generated NEF/NCP notifications through kycourts.net eFiling.
    77|- In an elderly MVA case (DOI 1/2022), the case had a prior $800 offer before the firm's involvement. EvenUP was used for case valuation. Despite extensive treatment (21+ providers including specialists), the demand was emailed to defense attorney during active litigation. The client's voicemail was full during case updates, creating communication challenges during litigation.
    78|- In a WC case (Ford Motor Company, DOI 5/2023), defense counsel scheduled client deposition in-person at their office and an IME. The client passed away (11/2/2025) before the deposition (scheduled 11/14/2025). Family member called to notify firm of client's death. The firm then notified defense counsel of the death. This represents a unique litigation event — client death during active WC proceedings requiring immediate procedural action.
    79|- In a multi-defendant auto accident case in litigation (Jefferson Circuit Court, 21-CI-004985, DOI 1/2021), the litigation spanned 4+ years (2021-2025+). Activities included: complaint filing, interrogatory responses to defense counsel, deposition scheduling with defense counsel, State Farm motion to dismiss (AOC 280 Final Submission for Adjudication), certified medical records collection via ChartSwap, government open records requests for police body cam footage (via Axon/Evidence.com), multiple ORRs to LMPD, amended subpoenas (to Verizon and Metro PCS for phone records), Louisville Metro public records requests, forwarding evidence to defense counsel (Dinsmore, Smith & Hoskins), and locating witnesses (other vehicle passengers). This is the most complex litigation case observed, involving multiple defense counsel, government records, and electronic evidence. The 4+ year timeline from DOI to ongoing litigation confirms that multi-defendant cases with uninsured drivers dramatically extend case duration.
    80|
    81|## Agent Guidance
    82|
    83|1. Create a litigation calendar at filing with all deadlines from the court's scheduling order
    84|2. Track every court notification (NEF, NCP) and file appropriately
    85|3. Maintain a discovery checklist: what's been requested, what's been produced, what's outstanding
    86|4. For supplemental discovery responses, track what new information needs to be disclosed
    87|5. Coordinate expert scheduling well in advance — FCE and IME providers have limited availability
    88|6. When mediator sends a Zoom link or scheduling confirmation, immediately distribute to all parties
    89|7. Monitor motion hearing schedule — courts frequently reschedule
    90|8. Keep a running litigation expense tracker (expert fees, filing fees, mediator fees)
    91|
    92|## Pitfalls
    93|
    94|- Missing court deadlines (can result in sanctions or adverse rulings)
    95|- Not tracking supplemental discovery obligations
    96|- Allowing expert reports to become stale (e.g., new treatment occurred after IME)
    97|- Not verifying certified records meet litigation requirements
    98|- Failing to respond to mediator scheduling promptly (can delay case months)
    99|- Not updating the trial calendar when deadlines change

@@ In Case H (MVA), initial intake @@

+- In Case N (premises liability estate, DOI 7/2020), the injured client died and the surviving spouse became the sole point of contact for the estate case. The spouse handled all communications, called for updates repeatedly, completed probate inventory, and relayed information from the home health provider that corrected a records request error (wrong provider name). The spouse showed classic frustration escalation — calling for updates, being told the attorney would call back, then calling again when no callback came. This is a distinct variant of the family-as-contact pattern: when the client has died, the family member is not just a proxy but the actual party (as estate representative), adding both legal and emotional complexity.
*** Update File: /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/case-manager-handoff-patterns.md

@@ In multiple cases @@
 - In multiple cases, the same paralegal (Sarena) handled the case from intake through years of treatment/litigation, only for a different staff member (Bryce) to take over later phases. The CC pattern during transition helped maintain continuity.
+- In Case M (MVA, DOI 8/2023), the case was touched by at least 4 staff members over its lifecycle: one attorney handled intake and initial client contact, a paralegal handled file setup and records requests, then the lead attorney managed demand and negotiation, and finally a different attorney took over litigation (depositions, mediation scheduling). The handoff from negotiation to litigation coincided with the case moving from inter-counsel policy distribution to formal suit. The client's frustration ("does not receive call backs or is told conflicting information repeatedly") was explicitly attributed to multiple staff giving different information, confirming that handoff-related inconsistency is a direct client frustration trigger.
+- In Case N (premises liability estate, DOI 7/2020), the case spanned 5+ years and was touched by multiple staff. The paralegal (Sarena) handled litigation tasks (discovery responses, filing) while attorneys handled case strategy. The case was referred out to outside counsel (Joel Franklin) at one point, then continued with the original firm. The surviving spouse's repeated calls for updates that went unanswered suggest communication gaps during the handoff periods. This confirms that 5+ year cases will inevitably experience multiple handoffs and each one creates a communication risk.
*** Update File: /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/connections/premises-liability-requires-litigation-prep.md
@@ evidence_count @@
-evidence_count: 1
+evidence_count: 2
@@ Had the team anticipated @@
 In the observed PL case, the case spanned 6+ years from incident to present, involving complaint filing, discovery, depositions, mediation, and multiple expert evaluations. The team had to re-request records as certified copies for litigation and coordinate multiple expert evaluations that were rescheduled repeatedly. Had the team anticipated litigation from the start, certified records could have been requested initially, and expert evaluations could have been scheduled earlier in the process.
+
+In Case N (premises liability estate, fall at hospital, DOI 7/2020), the case confirmed this pattern: the litigation spanned 5+ years with full discovery (interrogatories, RFAs, document production). Records from a home health provider were misidentified at intake (wrong provider name), causing wasted effort. Bills for the home health provider remained outstanding 3+ years into litigation. An expert consultant was retained for case evaluation. The case was even referred to specialist litigation counsel at one point. Had the team prepared for litigation from day one — requesting certified records, correctly identifying all providers, and engaging expert consultants earlier — the 5+ year timeline could potentially have been compressed.
*** Update File: /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/connections/client-frustration-drives-premature-settlement.md

-evidence_count: 4
+evidence_count: 6
@@ the attorney's prompt @@
 In the premises liability case (6+ years in litigation), the client texted the attorney directly after a court appearance with no follow-up. The attorney's prompt, substantive response prevented escalation — demonstrating that responsive communication, even when there's limited news, maintains the relationship.
+
+In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff), the client's frustration was driven by a factor outside the firm's control: another plaintiff's counsel failing to provide medical bills needed for policy limits distribution. The client called repeatedly and was told "we are waiting on the other firm." The frustration escalated to an in-person office visit. This variant shows that inter-counsel coordination delays create the same frustration dynamic as internal delays — and the client cannot distinguish between the two.
+
+In Case N (premises liability estate, 5+ year litigation), the surviving spouse called repeatedly over 18+ months (Jul 2025-Mar 2026) with missed calls and unreturned messages. The emotional stakes are higher in estate cases where the contact is grieving. The long litigation timeline without visible progress compounds the frustration. Despite the firm actively conducting litigation (depositions, discovery), the spouse perceived inaction because he was not being proactively updated.
*** End Patch"}

Had the team anticipated litigation from the start, certified records could have been requested initially, and expert evaluations could have been scheduled earlier in the process.
 
+In Case N (premises liability estate, fall at hospital, DOI 7/2020), the case confirmed this pattern: the litigation spanned 5+ years with full discovery (interrogatories, RFAs, document production). Records from a home health provider were misidentified at intake (wrong provider name), causing wasted effort. Bills for the home health provider remained outstanding 3+ years into litigation. An expert consultant was retained for case evaluation. The case was even referred to specialist litigation counsel at one point. Had the team prepared for litigation from day one — requesting certified records, correctly identifying all providers, and engaging expert consultants earlier — the 5+ year timeline could potentially have been compressed.
+
 ## Agent Guidance
 
 1. For all premises liability cases, request CERTIFIED records from the start (even before litigation is filed)
